revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Inbe clan]][[fa:خاندان اینبه]][[ja:忌部氏]][[pl:Inbe]][[simple:Inbe clan]][[th:ตระกูลอิมเบะ]][[zh:忌部氏]]
{{Short description|Japanese clan during the Yamato period}} {{Expand language|otherarticle=忌部氏|langcode=ja|date=October 2021}} {{Infobox Japanese clan|image=File:Precincts of Awa-jinja.JPG|founder=[[Futodama]]}} '''Inbe clan''' (also spelled '''Imibe clan''' or '''Inbe clan''')<ref>{{Cite book|last=Hardacre |first=Helen|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ|title=Shinto: A History|date=2017 |publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|pages=64}}</ref> was a Japanese clan during the {{ill|Yamato period|en|Yamato period|qid=Q633881|lt=Yamato period}}.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Frédéric|first1=Louis |title=Japan Encyclopedia|date=2002|publisher=Harvard University Press |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&q=Imbe+clan&pg=PA384 |isbn=978-0-674-01753-5 |pages=384}}</ref> They claimed descent from [[Futodama]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto 詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9415|access-date=2021-09-21|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja}}</ref>
The Inbe clan originally had a religious function by preparing and taking care of offerings.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Breen|first1=John|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=DR2PAQAAQBAJ&q=Inbe+clan&pg=PA49 |title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami|last2=Teeuwen|first2=Mark|date=2013-10-18|publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-82697-9|pages=49}}</ref>
According to the [[Kogo Shūi]] the Inbe clan were given {{ill|Awa Province (Tokushima)|en|Awa Province (Tokushima)|qid=Q907457|lt=Awa Province}} in [[Shikoku]] so they could cultivate hemp there.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Ab |first=Weiner |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=D_RvDwAAQBAJ&q=Imbe+clan&pg=PA384 |title=CLOTH & HUMAN EXP PB |date=1991-04-17 |publisher=Smithsonian Institution |isbn=978-0-87474-995-3 |pages=386}}</ref>{{Rp|page=384}} This is where {{ill|Inbe Shrine|en|Inbe Shrine|qid=Q11490722|lt=Inbe Shrine}} was built.
Their family shrines are [[Awa Shrine]]<ref>{{Citation |last=Carter |first=Caleb |title=Imagining an Ancient Tradition: Eighteenth-Century Narratives of Shugendō at Mount Togakushi |date=2020 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781350179424.ch-003 |work=Defining Shugendō |access-date=2023-10-27 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-350-17939-4}}</ref> and {{ill|Inbe Shrine|en|Inbe Shrine|qid=Q11490722|lt=Inbe Shrine}}.
== Overview == {{Unreferenced section|date=October 2021}} As the name of the clan "Imu" means "to abstain from [[Kegare]]", or "Saibai", the Imu clan was responsible for the rituals of the ancient Imperial Court, as well as for making ritual implements and building palaces. In the narrow sense, it refers to the Inbe family, the central family that led the Inbe clan, but in the broad sense, it includes the clans of the tribesmen who were led by the Inbe clan.
The main Inbe family claimed its ancestor was [[Futodama|Amatatama-no-mikoto]], who appeared in the [[Amano-Iwato]] myth of the Chronicles. It was based in the area around the present-day town of Inbe in {{ill|Kashihara, Nara|en|Kashihara, Nara|qid=Q496319|lt=Kashihara, Nara}}. They led the various clans in the region, and together with the [[Nakatomi clan]], administered rituals for the Imperial Court since ancient times. In the [[Norito|Shukushi]] of the [[Engishiki|Enki-Shiki]], it is written, "Let the Saibe clan's shukushi be used for the rituals of the palace and the gates, and let the Nakatomi clan's shukushi be used for all other rituals."
However, starting around the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}, the Nakatomi clan grew in power, and overwhelmed the Inbe position. In the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, the clan changed its name from Inbe to Saibe, and Saibe Hironari wrote the book ''[[Kogo Shūi]]''. However, it never regained its momentum, and the position of ritual clan was occupied by the Nakatomi and Ohnakatomi clans.
The Inbe were divided into two groups: the Tomobe (public officials belonging to the imperial court) and the Kakibe (private citizens of the Inbe). Unlike the Saibes of the central clans, who had fewer and fewer achievements, the Inbe of the various regions included Izumo, who delivered jade, Kii, who delivered wood, Awa, who delivered cotton and linen, and {{ill|Sanuki Province|en|Sanuki Province|qid=Q929682|lt=Sanuki}}, who delivered shields. It is known from the literature that the people of these tribes later took the name of Inbe. These local clans left their traces everywhere.
== Origins == {{Unreferenced section|date=October 2021}} In the [[Kojiki]] and the [[Nihon Shoki]], it is written that ''[[Futodama|Amenotama-no-mikoto]]'' and [[Ame-no-Koyane|Amenokoya-no-mikoto]] were involved in rituals in the myth of [[Amano-Iwato]]. Ame-no-Koyane were involved in the ritual relationship, and both deities were followed together in the [[Tenson kōrin|Descent of the Sun]]. Both of these deities were in charge of the rituals of the Imperial Court at the time of the compilation of the Chronicles. This reflects the difference in power between the Nakatomi clan and the Inbe clan at the time of the compilation. On the contrary, the position is reversed in the [[Kogo Shūi]] of the Inbe clan.
The [[Kogo Shūi]] on the side of the Inbe clan reverses its position. As for the origin of Amata-tamamikoto, the [[Kogo Shūi]] says that he is the son of [[Takamimusubi|Takamimusubi no Kami]], and the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|qid=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Surname Records}} follows this, but the origin is not mentioned in the [[Kojiki]] or [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]], so the truth is not clear.
== History == The clan started off as low class but gained power due to religious reasons.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Imbe Clan • A History of Japan - 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/imbe-clan/|access-date=2021-09-20|website=A History of Japan - 日本歴史}}</ref> During the reign of [[Emperor Kōtoku]], the Inbe, along with the [[Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi]] and Urabe clans, were tasked with supervising [[Department of Divinities]].<ref name=":0" /> The clan lost its power during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|qid=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu}}.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Brown|first1=Delmer M.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C&q=Inbe+clan&pg=PA518|title=The Cambridge History of Japan|last2=Hall |first2=John Whitney|last3=McCullough|first3=William H.|last4=Jansen|first4=Marius B. |last5=Shively|first5=Donald H.|last6=Yamamura|first6=Kozo|last7=Duus|first7=Peter|date=1988 |publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-0-521-22352-2|pages=518}}</ref> In the year 927 CE, members of the clan lost their long-standing right to present ''[[Hemp#Japan|asa]]'' cloth for use in the imperial rituals.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Ab|first=Weiner|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=D_RvDwAAQBAJ&q=Imbe+clan&pg=PA384|title=CLOTH & HUMAN EXP PB|date=1991-04-17|publisher=Smithsonian Institution|isbn=978-0-87474-995-3|pages=386}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
